one_line_takeaway: A purpose-built CNN-LSTM over 8-grams of opcodes and native API calls edges out fourteen fine-tuned ViT/CNN backbones at ~99.9% Windows-malware family classification, but the eye-popping accuracy, an under-specified feature-extraction pipeline, and internal table inconsistencies warrant caution about leakage and reproducibility.
key_finding: The custom CNN-LSTM-3 with 8-gram opcode+API features reaches 99.91% average accuracy (Table 3: max 99.98, min 99.90, loss 0.0014), narrowly beating the best fine-tuned transfer-learning backbones Swin-T (99.82% avg) and Sequencer2D-L (99.70% avg), and far exceeding weaker backbones such as ConvNeXt-T (85.30%) and MaxViT-B (85.07%). The 8-gram representation is decisively best: for CNN-LSTM-3, accuracy at other n ranges only ~57-78% versus 99.91% at n=8 (Table 9). ANOVA reports CNN-LSTM-3 as most statistically significant (p=0.002 vs Swin-T p=0.005, Sequencer2D-L p=0.007).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is twofold. (1) A specific hybrid CNN-LSTM classifier, called CNN-LSTM-3, for multi-class Windows malware family classification. Its novelty is not the generic idea of pairing a CNN with an LSTM (that is credited in the paper's own Related Work to Zhang [15], who used CNN-LSTM over n-grams of API calls), but rather a concrete architecture (3 convolutional layers with 9x9 kernels and ELU activation, 3 max-pooling layers with 3x3 kernels, 3 stacked LSTM layers of 512 units, dropout 0.3, one dense softmax head over 20 classes) combined with a dual feature representation: X is a one-hot encoding of 8-grams of opcodes/API calls, and Y is a concatenation of Bag-of-Words and TF-IDF representations of those 8-grams. It also extracts not only regular API calls but native/undocumented Windows APIs (Nt*/Zw* prefixes) plus opcode sequences. (2) An empirical transfer-learning benchmark that fine-tunes fourteen recent pre-trained vision/sequence backbones on the same features and compares them; those backbones (ConvNeXt-T/S, RegNetY-4/8/12GF, EfficientNetV2, Sequencer2D-L, ViT-G/14, ViT-Ti/S/B/L, MaxViT-B, Swin-T) are prior work the paper merely fine-tunes, not architectures it invents.
threat_model: Defensive multi-class classification/triage of Windows malware. The operational context is that malware authors use evasion techniques to avoid anti-virus signatures, so the system aims to classify samples into malware families (and benign) from static opcode sequences plus dynamic/native API-call features. The work is a detector/classifier, not an attack. It evaluates no adversarial robustness: there is no test against evasion, obfuscation, or adversarial-example attacks against the classifier itself, despite evasion being cited as the motivation.
limitations: (1) The reported training-set size is printed as the malformed '9,749,57'; the 19 named family counts in Table 1 sum to exactly 974,957, the evident intended value (plus 89,004 benign). (2) The paper says it classifies '20 malware families', but the 20 classes are 19 malware families + benign. (3) Internal numeric inconsistencies: Table 3 gives CNN-LSTM-3 precision/recall/F1 of 99.88/99.89/99.87 while Table 11 gives 99.62/99.62/99.62 (AUC 99.62), and the prose near the results is garbled ('0.01, 99.84, 99.91, 99.99, 99.87'); the conclusion cites Swin-T at 99.83% vs 99.82% in Table 7. (4) Feature extraction is under-specified and unusual: Postman and SoapUI are API-testing tools, not malware sandboxes, and how they yield opcodes/native API traces is not explained. (5) How opcode/API text is converted into image input for the vision backbones (ViTs, ConvNeXt, Swin, etc.) is never clearly described. (6) Near-perfect ~99.9% accuracy on n-gram features raises data-leakage/overfitting concerns, especially with samples drawn from the same sources for train and test. (7) Table 8's comparison to external published methods (LSTM 95%, BERT 96.76%, CNN 98.82%, etc.) is on different datasets and explicitly acknowledged as not directly comparable. (8) A confusing claim in the Discussion states Swin-T and Sequencer2D-L were 'only pre-trained using a malware dataset', which contradicts the ImageNet-pretrained-then-fine-tuned methodology.

datasets_used: Authors' own collected dataset: printed as '9,749,57' malware samples (Table 1 family counts sum to 974,957) + 89,004 benign, from VirusShare and VirusTotal (malware 2019-2023, benign 2021-2023), spanning 19 malware families (e.g., Dyfuca 97,563; Ryuk 91,205; Mytob 86,988; Agobot 84,091; IstBar 74,338; Limr 73,451; WanaCrypt0r 70,001) + benign, i.e. 20 classes
Public test datasets for cross-evaluation: VirusSamples, MalShare, VirusTotal, Dynamite AI Lab, The Zoo (GitHub)
quantitative_results: CNN-LSTM-3: 99.91% avg accuracy (Table 3: max 99.98, min 99.90, loss 0.0014; precision 99.88, recall 99.89, F1 99.87)
CNN-LSTM-3 alternate metrics (Table 11): precision/recall/F1 99.62, AUC 99.62 (inconsistent with Table 3)
Swin-T (fine-tuned): 99.82% avg (max 99.94, min 99.79); Sequencer2D-L: 99.70% avg (max 99.73, min 99.66)
ViT-G/14: 93.12% avg; ViT-Ti: 91.72%; RegNetY-4GF: 91.16%; EfficientNetV2: 90.54%; VIT-B: 90.04%; ConvNeXt-T: 85.30%; MaxViT-B: 85.07% (lowest)
Intra-paper baselines (Table 3): GRU 89.29%, RNN 84.72%, plain CNN 89.12%, CNN-LSTM-1 90.82%, CNN-LSTM-2 90.99%, CNN-LSTM-4 90.44%
Cross-dataset (Table 12) CNN-LSTM-3: VirusSamples 99.89%, MalShare 99.89%, VirusTotal 99.90%, Dynamite AI Lab 99.90%, The Zoo 99.88%
ANOVA (Table 10): CNN-LSTM-3 p=0.002, Swin-T p=0.005, Sequencer2D-L p=0.007; ConvNeXt-T p=0.325 (not significant)
baselines_compared: In-paper recurrent/conv baselines: GRU, RNN, plain CNN, LSTM configs (CNN-LSTM-1/2/4 at 128/256/1024 LSTM units)
Fourteen fine-tuned pre-trained backbones: ConvNeXt-T, ConvNeXt-S, RegNetY-4GF, RegNetY-8GF, RegNetY-12GF, EfficientNetV2, Sequencer2D-L, ViT-G/14, ViT-Ti, ViT-S, ViT-B, ViT-L, MaxViT-B, Swin-T

External published methods on different datasets (Table 8, explicitly not directly comparable): LSTM 95%, Gradient Classifier 94.64%, CNN-BiLSTM 96.76%, CNN 98.82%, BiLSTM 93.16%, TextCNN 95.90%, RNN 91%, BERT 96.76%
